real_world_anchor.primary: Dragging a heavy suitcase up a ramp at Mumbai Central station — rope pulls at an angle, but only the horizontal part actually moves it forward.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A kite string in the wind — the tension in the string has a horizontal pull and a vertical lift, and both matter.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A farmer's bullock ploughing a field — the yoke applies force at an angle; only the horizontal component does useful work.

locked_facts: A vector at angle alpha from the x-axis has component F*cos(alpha) along x and F*sin(alpha) along y.
Components are always perpendicular to each other.
The magnitude of the vector equals the square root of (Fx^2 + Fy^2) — Pythagoras.
Cosine pairs with the axis you measured the angle FROM.
Choosing axes aligned with motion simplifies the equations — never a wrong choice, just a smart one.

STATE_1.title: Why Split a Vector? The Trolley Question

scene_composition.text:
You pull with 10 N at 50°.
The trolley only moves along the ground.
How much of your pull actually moves it forward?
O

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is a question that trips up almost every physics student.
You pull a trolley with a rope at an angle — say fifty degrees above the ground.
You are applying ten Newtons of force. But the trolley only moves forward, along the ground.
So how much of your ten Newton pull actually moves it? Ten? Half? Something else?
The answer lies in a powerful idea called resolving a vector. Let us see it.

scene_composition.text:
From the arrow's tip,
drop a perpendicular to each axis.
Where the shadow lands
— that is the component.

The original arrow is just
the diagonal of the rectangle
made by its two components.

tts_sentences.text_en: Watch what happens when we draw two perpendicular lines through the point — one along the ground, one straight up.
From the tip of the arrow, drop a perpendicular onto each line. Two dashed shadows appear.
Each shadow is called a component. The horizontal shadow tells you how much of the pull moves the trolley forward.
The vertical shadow tells you how much of the pull is trying to lift it off the ground.
And the original arrow? It is just the diagonal of the rectangle formed by its components. This is the geometry of resolution.

STATE_3.title: The Right Triangle — Where sin and cos Live

scene_composition.text: hypotenuse = F

Focus on the right triangle.
The arrow is the HYPOTENUSE.
The components are the two SIDES.

ADJACENT to α → horizontal side.
OPPOSITE to α → vertical side.
Trigonometry is built
exactly for this shape.

tts_sentences.text_en: Look closely at the geometry here. The arrow, its two shadows, and the axes form a rectangle. The arrow is the diagonal.
Now focus on just half of this rectangle — the right triangle sitting on the x-axis.
The arrow itself is the hypotenuse. The horizontal component is the side ADJACENT to angle alpha. The vertical component is the side OPPOSITE to alpha.
Why does this matter so much? Because trigonometry is built for exactly this shape. Sine and cosine are about to pop out.

STATE_4.title: The Formula — F cosα and F sinα

scene_composition.text:
MEMORY RULE:
cos stays WITH the axis
you measured α FROM.
sin goes with the perpendicular.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the formulas. In any right triangle, cosine of the angle equals ADJACENT over hypotenuse.
So the adjacent side equals hypotenuse times cosine. That gives us Fx equals F times cosine alpha.
Similarly, sine equals opposite over hypotenuse. So Fy equals F times sine alpha.
Memorize this rule and you will never mix them up: cosine goes with the axis you measured the angle FROM.
Let us verify. Ten Newtons at fifty degrees: cosine fifty is zero point six four, so Fx is six point four three Newtons. Sine fifty is zero point seven seven, so Fy is seven point six six Newtons.
And by Pythagoras, the square root of six point four three squared plus seven point six six squared equals ten. The vector is preserved — nothing is lost.

STATE_5.title: A Real Problem — The Block on an Incline

scene_composition.text:
Block on a 30° incline.
Gravity pulls straight down:
mg = 2 × 9.8 = 19.6 N

The block will SLIDE.
But how much of this 19.6 N
is actually sliding it down?

Which axes should we resolve mg along?

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us apply this skill to a classic physics problem. A block of mass two kilograms sits on a thirty-degree incline.
Gravity pulls it straight down. The red arrow shows m g equals nineteen point six Newtons, pointing to the center of the earth.
The block will slide down the ramp. But here is the question: how much of that nineteen point six Newtons is actually doing the sliding?
The answer depends entirely on how we resolve m g. Which pair of axes should we pick? Let us try two different choices and see.

STATE_6.title: Meet the Axes — What 'World x and y' Actually Mean

scene_composition.text:
Before resolving anything:
WHAT axes are we resolving along?

'World x and y' = the fixed
horizontal and vertical of the room.

KEY POINT:
These axes do NOT rotate
when you tilt the incline.
They stay fixed to the LAB,
not to the block or the ramp.

We CAN resolve mg along these
world axes — or along ANY other pair
of perpendicular axes.

Choosing well is the whole game.

tts_sentences.text_en: Before we can resolve m g along anything, we need to be very clear about what 'the axes' actually are.

Look at the bottom-left of the screen. Those two gray arrows are the world x and y axes. World x is horizontal — parallel to the floor of your room. World y is vertical — pointing straight up, against gravity.

Here is the key point that confuses many students. These world axes do NOT rotate when you place a block on an incline. They stay fixed to the laboratory — to the room itself — not to the block, not to the ramp.
So we have a choice. We can resolve m g along these fixed world axes. Or we can pick a different pair of perpendicular axes — any pair we like.
Choosing the right pair is the whole game. Let us try the obvious choice first: the world axes you already know.

STATE_7.title: First Try — World x and y. It Fails.

scene_composition.text:
FIRST TRY: resolve mg along
those gray world axes
(the ones you just met).

Watch the gray arrows:
• Horizontal component = 0
• Vertical component = mg itself

We learned NOTHING new.

Why? The BLOCK moves
along the incline — not vertically.
Our axes don't match the motion.

tts_sentences.text_en: First, try the obvious choice. Resolve m g along the world x axis and the y axis — the axes you already know from the previous states.
Watch the gray arrows appear. The horizontal component is zero, because m g has no horizontal part — it points straight down.
And the vertical component just equals m g itself. We have not learned anything new at all.
So why did this fail? Look at the motion. The block slides ALONG the incline, not vertically. Our axes do not match the motion. That is the whole problem.

STATE_8.title: Second Try — Rotate the Axes. Physics Becomes Simple.

scene_composition.text:
SECOND TRY:
Rotate the axes to match the incline.
One axis along the slope,
one perpendicular to it.

ALONG the slope:
mg sinθ = 9.80 N
→ pulls the block down the ramp.
THIS is the sliding force.

PERPENDICULAR:
mg cosθ = 16.97 N
→ pushes block into surface.
Surface pushes back → that IS N.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now the clever move. Rotate our axes so one matches the incline and the other is perpendicular to it.
Watch the green dashed arrows appear. Suddenly m g splits into two brand new pieces.

The first piece is along the slope. Its magnitude is m g times sine theta — which equals nine point eight zero Newtons. That is the force pulling the block down the ramp. That is exactly what makes it slide.
The second piece is perpendicular to the incline. Its magnitude is m g times cosine theta — sixteen point nine seven Newtons. That is the force pushing the block into the surface.

The surface pushes back with the same magnitude. That pushback is called the normal reaction, N. And now you know where the famous formula N equals m g cosine theta comes from — it is just the perpendicular component of gravity.
The lesson is permanent. Pick axes that match the motion. Resolve the forces along them. Physics becomes simple.

scene_composition.text:
TRY THESE:
• α = 0°  → all force horizontal
• α = 90° → all force vertical
• α = 45° → Fx = Fy, both smaller than F
• Double F → components double

You will resolve vectors
thousands of times in physics.
Forces, velocities, accelerations —
the skill never changes.
Now you own it.

tts_sentences.text_en: Now it is your turn. Drag the sliders and watch the components respond.
Set alpha to zero. The whole vector becomes horizontal. F cos alpha equals F. F sine alpha is zero.
Set alpha to ninety. The whole vector becomes vertical. Now F sine alpha equals F.
At forty-five degrees, both components are exactly equal — and each one is smaller than F itself. Seven point zero seven, to be exact.
Change the magnitude F. Notice both components scale together. The angle sets the RATIO. The magnitude sets the SIZE.
This is the core skill of mechanics. Every force, every velocity, every acceleration you meet — you will resolve it just like this. Now you know how.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student swaps sine and cosine — thinks Fx = F sinα when α is measured from x-axis.
trigger_phrases: isn't Fx equal to F sin
why not F sin alpha for horizontal
i thought sine was the x component

EPIC_C_1.title: Check: Which Side of the Triangle Is Which?

scene_composition.text: Triangle anatomy — which side is adjacent?

cos(α) = adjacent / hypotenuse
So adjacent = F * cos(α).
The adjacent side IS the x-axis here —
hence Fx = F * cos(α).

tts_sentences.text_en: Pause — this is a very common confusion. Let us settle it forever.
In a right triangle, cosine equals adjacent over hypotenuse. Sine equals opposite over hypotenuse.
The angle alpha is measured from the x-axis. So the x-axis is the side NEXT to the angle. That is the adjacent side.
Adjacent pairs with cosine. That means x pairs with cosine. Always.

EPIC_C_2.title: The Safe Rule — cos with the Axis You Measured FROM

left_scene.text:
Fx = F cos(α)  (with x, from which we measured)
Fy = F sin(α)  (perpendicular)

right_scene.text:
Fy = F cos(α)  (with y, from which we measured)
Fx = F sin(α)  (perpendicular)

scene_composition.equation: cos pairs with the axis you measured FROM.  sin pairs with the perpendicular.

scene_composition.text: One sentence. Never mix them up again.

tts_sentences.text_en: If you ever forget, use this one rule: cosine goes with the axis you measured the angle FROM.
Measured from x? Then Fx is F cos alpha, Fy is F sin alpha.
Measured from y? Then Fy is F cos alpha, Fx is F sin alpha.
It is always cosine with the axis you measured from. Never the other way around.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student sees angle drawn from y-axis but still writes Fx = F cos(α).

EPIC_C_1.title: When the Given Angle Is Measured from the y-axis

scene_composition.equation:
Fy = F * cos(α)   (aligned with y — the axis we measured FROM)
Fx = F * sin(α)   (perpendicular to y)

scene_composition.text:
Trap: students write Fx = F cos(α)
by habit. Wrong here —
because α is measured from y, not x.

tts_sentences.text_en: In many problems, the given angle is measured from the y-axis or from a surface — not from the x-axis.
When that happens, cosine pairs with y, because y is the axis you measured from.
So Fy equals F cosine alpha. Fx equals F sine alpha — the perpendicular component.
Always look at the diagram first and identify which axis the angle is drawn from. That single check saves most of the sin-cos mistakes in exam papers.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student thinks Fx + Fy equals F arithmetically (as if vectors added like numbers).
trigger_phrases: why Fx + Fy not equal to F

EPIC_C_1.title: Why Fx + Fy ≠ F — Pythagoras, Not Arithmetic

scene_composition.text:
Components are perpendicular.
To recover F, use Pythagoras —
not arithmetic addition.

tts_sentences.text_en: A common check goes wrong here. Students compute Fx plus Fy and expect it to equal F.
For F equals ten newtons at fifty degrees, Fx is six point four three and Fy is seven point six six. Their sum is fourteen point zero nine — not ten.
That is not a mistake; it is the whole point of vectors. The two components are perpendicular, so they cannot be added like ordinary numbers.
The correct way to recover F is Pythagoras: square root of Fx squared plus Fy squared. That always brings you back to ten.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Student believes axes must always be horizontal and vertical — you cannot rotate them.
trigger_phrases: axes must be horizontal and vertical
is it allowed to rotate axes

EPIC_C_1.title: Your Belief: Axes Must Be World xy

scene_composition.text: Myth: axes must match the room.

On an incline, forcing world x–y
leaves mg with two ugly components
neither of which is along the motion.
Result: messy algebra.

tts_sentences.text_en: Many students assume axes must always be horizontal and vertical — that the room fixes them forever.
That is not a rule of physics. It is a habit from early drawings.
On an incline problem, forcing world axes leaves mg with two awkward components, neither of which points along the direction of motion.
The algebra becomes ugly. That is the price of the wrong axis choice.

EPIC_C_2.title: Reality: Pick Any Perpendicular Pair You Like

scene_composition.text: Truth: ANY perpendicular pair is valid.

scene_composition.equation:
Along incline: mg sin(θ) — drives motion
Perpendicular: mg cos(θ) — balanced by N

scene_composition.text:
Rule: choose axes that align with the motion
or the constraint. The algebra becomes trivial.

tts_sentences.text_en: The rule is simple. Any two perpendicular directions can serve as axes. Physics does not care whether they are horizontal and vertical or tilted.
On an incline, rotate the axes to match the slope. One along the incline, one perpendicular to it.
Now mg splits cleanly. Along the incline it equals mg sine theta — the force that drives the sliding. Perpendicular it equals mg cosine theta — the force balanced by the normal reaction.
Choosing axes that match the motion is not a trick. It is a right you always have — and using it is the difference between easy physics and painful algebra.

regeneration_variants.label: Kite in the Wind
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A child flies a kite. The string pulls down at an angle — part lifts the child's hand, part pulls it forward.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Vector tension resolved into horizontal and vertical components.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Boy holding kite string; string at 60°; T cosα horizontal pull, T sinα vertical pull.

regeneration_variants.label: Cricket Bat Swing Velocity
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A batsman's bat velocity at impact has horizontal and vertical parts; only the angle of the bat determines where the ball goes.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Velocity vector resolved into horizontal and vertical components.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: Bat making contact; velocity vector decomposed on swing path.

regeneration_variants.label: Ship Against Current
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A ferry crosses a river with current. The engine thrust at an angle to the bank gives forward speed and sideways drift.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Velocity vector resolved into along-river and across-river components.
regeneration_variants.scene_hint: River banks; ferry; engine arrow at angle; two velocity components.

trigger_phrases: mg sin theta mg cos theta
why do we resolve along the incline
how do i know when to use sin or cos

STATE_1.description: Identify the vector F at angle α from a reference axis. State the goal: find components along two perpendicular directions.

STATE_3.description: Draw the right triangle: F is the hypotenuse; adjacent side lies along the axis you measured α from; opposite side is perpendicular.

STATE_4.description: Apply trigonometry: Fx = F*cos(α), Fy = F*sin(α). State the memory rule — cos pairs with the axis measured FROM.

STATE_8.description: For a block on incline at angle θ, resolve mg along rotated axes: mg*sin(θ) along the slope (drives motion), mg*cos(θ) perpendicular (balanced by N).

STATE_9.description: Verify Pythagoras: sqrt(Fx^2 + Fy^2) = F. Cross-check with a numerical example (e.g., F=10 N, α=50° -> Fx=6.43, Fy=7.66, sqrt = 10).

primitives.text: Step 1: Given vector F at angle α from the x-axis.
Step 2: Goal — find Fx (along x) and Fy (along y).
Step 3: Components must satisfy Fx^2 + Fy^2 = F^2.
+1 for setup
Step 4: Drop perpendiculars from tip to both axes -> right triangle.
Step 5: F is the hypotenuse; adjacent side = x-leg; opposite side = y-leg.
Step 6: Angle α sits between the hypotenuse and the adjacent side.
+1 for triangle identification
Step 7: cos(α) = adjacent / hypotenuse -> Fx = F*cos(α).
Step 8: sin(α) = opposite / hypotenuse -> Fy = F*sin(α).
Step 9: Memory rule: cos pairs with axis measured FROM.
+1 for formula
Step 10: On incline (angle θ), rotate axes: x along slope, y perpendicular.
Step 11: mg_along = mg*sin(θ)  — pulls block down the slope.
Step 12: mg_perp  = mg*cos(θ)  — balanced by normal reaction N.
+1 for incline resolution
Step 13: Numerical check — F = 10 N, α = 50 deg.
Step 15: sqrt(6.43^2 + 7.66^2) = sqrt(100) = 10 N. Recovered.
+1 for Pythagoras verification

shortcuts.rule: Resolve vector along x and y
shortcuts.formula: Fx = F*cos(α),  Fy = F*sin(α)   (α from x-axis)
shortcuts.use_when: Angle is given from the x-axis (or the horizontal/reference direction). Default rule.

shortcuts.rule: When the angle is given from the y-axis
shortcuts.use_when: Angle is drawn from the vertical or from a surface's perpendicular.
Block on a ramp. Axes chosen along/perp to the slope.
Sanity check, or when reconstructing the resultant of two perpendicular components.

shortcuts.formula: tan(α) = Fy / Fx  ->  α = atan2(Fy, Fx)
shortcuts.use_when: You have Fx and Fy and need the direction.

shortcuts.rule: Pick axes that align with motion
shortcuts.formula: Any two perpendicular directions are valid axes.
shortcuts.use_when: Incline, circular motion, constrained motion. Saves algebra.

edge_cases.title: Rope tied to a block, angle given with the surface
edge_cases.trap: Students assume the angle is with the horizontal when the diagram says 'with the surface' on an incline.
edge_cases.resolution: If α is measured with the surface (incline at θ), then the rope makes α + θ with the horizontal. Resolve carefully — draw the diagram before plugging.

edge_cases.title: Projectile launched at angle θ from horizontal
edge_cases.resolution: θ is from horizontal, so vx = v*cos(θ) and vy = v*sin(θ). Classic cos-sin trap; apply the 'cos with the axis you measured from' rule.

edge_cases.title: 3D vector — direction cosines
edge_cases.trap: Students apply 2D Fx = F*cos(α) without recognising the third dimension.
edge_cases.resolution: Use direction cosines: Fx = F*cos(α), Fy = F*cos(β), Fz = F*cos(γ) with cos^2(α) + cos^2(β) + cos^2(γ) = 1.

edge_cases.title: Vector with angle > 90 degrees
edge_cases.trap: Students treat all angles as acute and lose the sign on Fx or Fy.
edge_cases.resolution: Use atan2 and the actual cos/sin values (which can be negative). Do NOT take absolute values. The sign encodes the quadrant.

edge_cases.title: Rope tension on a hanging block at non-vertical string
edge_cases.trap: Students resolve the rope's tension along the block's axes instead of the rope's direction.
edge_cases.resolution: Tension always acts along the rope. Resolve T into horizontal and vertical components using the angle the rope makes with the vertical (or horizontal).
